coredata.dc:title: A Eu3+-inspired fluorescent carbon nanodot probe for the sensitive visualization of anthrax biomarker by integrating EDTA chelation
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Hongwei
Lu, Fengniu
Zhan, Xingxiao
Tian, Mingce
Yuan, Zhiqin
Lu, Chao
coredata.dc:description: The rapid and sensitive visualization of 2,6-dipicolinic acid (DPA, a unique anthrax biomarker) is essential to prevent anthrax disease or biological terrorist attack. In this study, a Eu3+-labeled ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid loaded hyperbranched polyethyleneimine carbon nanodot (hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+) nanoprobe has been proposed for the ratiometric DPA detection. The sensing mechanism is based on the rapid DPA-Eu3+ chelation within 30 s and subsequent enhanced fluorescence emission through the antenna effect. With the introduction of EDTA chelating unit, the resulted fluorescence of Eu3+-complex is greatly enhanced, which endows sensitive DPA perception. By employing hPEI-CD as the internal reference, ratiometric DPA sensing is realized with a good linearity in the concentration range from 1.0 to 100 nM, with a limit of detection of 190 pM (S/N = 3). The specific chelation affinity between Eu3+ and DPA provides satisfying selectivity over other amino acids and ions. Using nanoprobe-loaded polyvinylidene fluoride paper as the analytical device, point-of-care DPA visualization is achieved. Furthermore, the practical application of designed paper device is validated by the visual detection of metabolic DPA-release from Bacillus subtilis spores.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bacillus anthracis
2,6-Dipicolinic acid
Carbon nanodot
Fluorimetric sensing
Visualization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: DPA sensing in human serum samples
Bacterial spore assay and screening of metabolic DPA-release
Synthesis and characterization of hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanostructure
Establishment of ratiometric DPA sensing platform
Ponit-of-care DPA detection with paper device
Visualization of DPA release from B. subtilis spore germination

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of ratiometric DPA sensing using hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanoprobe.

The normalized absorption (blue line), normalized fluorescence excitation (black line) and emission (red line) spectra of hPEI-CDs solution (A). Inset image: photograph of hPEI-CDs solution under the illumination of 365 nm UV light. HRTEM images of hPEI-CDs (B) and hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanostructure (C). Inset pictures are the corresponding size-distribution histograms, each from 100 counts. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Zeta potential (A) and FT-IR spectra (B) of hPEI-CDs, hPEI-CD-EDTA and hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanoprobe. Time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra of CDs (C) from hPEI-CDs, hPEI-CD-EDTA, and hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanostructure. XPS spectra (D) of hPEI-CDs-EDTA-Eu3+ nanostructure.

Fluorescence emission spectra of Tris-HCl buffer (pH 9.0, 25 mM) diluted hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanoprobe solution upon adding various concentrations of DPA (A). The final concentrations of DPA from bottom to up are 0, 1, 5, 10, 30, 50, 100, 300, 500, 800 and 1000 nM, respectively. Inset image: plots of intensity ratio (I616/I440) versus the concentration of DPA ranging from 0 to 100 nM. Fluorescence response of the hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanoprobe toward 100 nM various interferents in the absence (gray pillar) and presence (red pillar) of 100 nM DPA (B). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Schematic diagram of the point-of-care visualization of DPA using hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanoprobe-loaded PVDF paper device (A). Fluorescence images of the paper-based visual sensor over different concentrations of DPA (from left to right: 0, 0.5, 5.0, 40, 100, and 500 μM, respectively) under 254 nm UV light illumination (B).

Scheme of the production of DPA from B. subtilis spores via Ala stimulation (A). Fluorescence emission spectra of hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanoprobe without (black line) and with the addition of spores only (red line), Ala only (blue line), and spores + Ala (green line), respectively (B). Fluorescence photographs of the paper-based visual sensor upon adding bacterial metabolites with different mass concentrations of spores under 254 nm UV light illumination (C). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Comparison of this work with some established fluorometric DPA detection methods. a

head.ce:title: A Eu3+-inspired fluorescent carbon nanodot probe for the sensitive visualization of anthrax biomarker by integrating EDTA chelation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, College of Chemistry, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing, 100029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Institute of Advanced Industrial Science and Technology (AIST), 1-1-1 Higashi, Tsukuba, 305-8565, Ibaraki, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Institute of Advanced Industrial Science and Technology (AIST)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Institute of Advanced Industrial Science and Technology (AIST), 1-1-1 Higashi, Tsukuba 305-8565, Ibaraki, Japan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University, Changsha, 410082, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rapid and sensitive visualization of 2,6-dipicolinic acid (DPA, a unique anthrax biomarker) is essential to prevent anthrax disease or biological terrorist attack. In this study, a Eu3+-labeled ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid loaded hyperbranched polyethyleneimine carbon nanodot (hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+) nanoprobe has been proposed for the ratiometric DPA detection. The sensing mechanism is based on the rapid DPA-Eu3+ chelation within 30 s and subsequent enhanced fluorescence emission through the antenna effect. With the introduction of EDTA chelating unit, the resulted fluorescence of Eu3+-complex is greatly enhanced, which endows sensitive DPA perception. By employing hPEI-CD as the internal reference, ratiometric DPA sensing is realized with a good linearity in the concentration range from 1.0 to 100 nM, with a limit of detection of 190 pM (S/N = 3). The specific chelation affinity between Eu3+ and DPA provides satisfying selectivity over other amino acids and ions. Using nanoprobe-loaded polyvinylidene fluoride paper as the analytical device, point-of-care DPA visualization is achieved. Furthermore, the practical application of designed paper device is validated by the visual detection of metabolic DPA-release from Bacillus subtilis spores.

ce:section.ce:para: As a globally important animal disease and zoonosis caused by Bacillus anthracis (B. anthracis), anthrax can lead to serious infection and even death when inhaling high concentration of B. anthracis spores within one day [1]. According to the recent reports, 2,6-dipicolinic acid (DPA) takes up 5–15% dry weight of the B. anthracis spores, and can act as a unique anthrax biomarker [2–4]. Thus, the exploitation of rapid and effective method for the visualization of B. anthracis spores is essential to prevent anthrax disease and/or biological terrorist attack [5]. The strong binding affinity, narrow emission bands, as well as the large stokes shift make lanthanide ion (e.g., Eu3+ and Tb3+)-based fluorescence approaches favorable for DPA sensing [6–9]. Despite of the successful DPA perception by associating with fluorescence spectrophotometer, the point-of-care visualization of DPA is still a challenge, partially due to the high limit of detection (LOD). Therefore, it is still appealing to exploit a facile method for sensing DPA with improved sensitivity.

To achieve high sensitivity and accuracy in fluorimetric analysis, ratiometric probes with built-in correction characters have been approvingly exploited, which simultaneously monitor fluorescence intensities of two isolated emission components [10,11]. Among them, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET)-inclusive ratiometric sensing systems are charming because of their ultrahigh sensitivity [12,13]. Fluorescent nanodots with high quantum yield and easy surface modification character have been widely applied in sensing and bioimaging based on FRET strategy [14–16]. Especially, fluorescent carbon nanodots (CDs) usually with bright blue emission could be an ideal energy donor [17–19]. In comparison with simple reference-based ratiometric systems, CD-comprised FRET probes generally show higher sensitivity. For example, the CD-gold nanocluster nanosatellite FRET system displays a LOD of 2.9 nM toward hydrogen peroxide, which is two-order of magnitudes lower than other ratiometric probes [20]. In spite of the development of few CD-based ratiometric DPA sensing systems [10], the relatively high LODs still hinder the rapid and sensitive visualization of DPA. Thus, the improvement of DPA detection sensitivity is essential to exploit DPA visualization platform [21,22].

As concluded in previous reports, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA)-involved lanthanide ion-DPA complex shows stronger fluorescence due to perfect coordination [23]. It is thus speculated that the introduction of EDTA to the sensing system would greatly enhance the detection sensitivity, which is theoretically possible. In this study, we proposed a Eu3+-labeled EDTA loaded hyperbranched polyethyleneimine carbon nanodot (hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+) FRET nanoprobe for the sensitive detection of DPA. The hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanoprobes were synthesized by simple surface functionalization of hPEI-CDs. By employing hPEI-CD as the internal reference and EDTA-Eu3+ chelating complex as the sensing element, ratiometric DPA detection was achieved. The schematic representation of DPA sensing principle was shown in Fig. 1 With the introduction of EDTA chelating unit, the proposed nanoprobe exhibited high sensitivity toward DPA with a LOD of 190 pM (S/N = 3). The specificity toward DPA over other amino acids, anions and metal ions was also investigated. With such a high sensitivity, the point-of-care DPA detection in nanoprobe-loaded polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) paper device was realized as expected. Moreover, the practical application of designed paper device was validated by the visual screening of metabolic DPA release from Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis) spores.

2,6-Dipicolinic acid (DPA) and ethylenediaminetetraacetic dianhydride (EDTAD) were purchased from adamas-beta (Shanghai, China). Citric acid (CA) and hyperbranched polyethyleneimine (hPEI, 1800Da) were obtained from Alfa Aesar (Ward Hill, MA, USA). Potassium chloride (KCl), potassium nitrate (KNO3), sodium chloride (NaCl), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), and calcium chloride (CaCl2) were obtained from Beijing chemical (Beijing, China). Benzoic acid (BA) was purchased from Fuchen chemical (Tianjin, China), d-aspartic acid (Asp), glutamic acid (Glu), alanine (Ala), and cysteine (Cys) were purchased from Solarbio (Beijing, China). Europium nitrate hexahydrate (Eu(NO3)3middot6H2O) was purchased from HWRK CHEM (Beijing, China). Tryptone was purchased from OXOID (Shanghai, China). Beef meal was purchased from AOBOX (Beijing, China). Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis, CCTCC AB 90008) were purchased from China Center for Type Culture Collection (CCTCC, Wuhan, China). All chemicals were used without further purification. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ) was obtained from a Millipore system. Tris-HCl buffer (0.1 M pH = 9.0) and carbonate buffer (0.1 M pH = 9.6) were prepared according to a standard handbook.

UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-2401PC spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were measured using a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained by using a Nicolet 6700 infrared spectrophotometer (Thermo, USA). Hydrodynamic diameter analysis and zeta potential measurements were performed with a Zetasizer Nano-ZS (Malvern, U.K.). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were collected on an ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo, USA). High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were performed on a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). The time-resolved fluorescence decay curve was performed on a FLS 980 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh, U.K.). The Au NCs solution was dried using a FD5-series freezedryer (SIM, USA).

The hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanostructure was prepared by a two-steps surface functionalization of hPEI-CDs. Firstly, hPEI-CDs were synthesized by a reported hydrothemal method with slight modifications [20,24]. Typically, 1.0 g CA and 0.6 g hPEI were dissolved with 10 mL hot water and was evaporated to form gel at 200 °C. After that, 1.0 mL ultrapure water was added into the resulted pale-yellow gel and the mixture was scorched through hydrothermal heating. The color of the gel was turned to orange after the procedure was repeated over 8 times in 3 h, revealing the formation of hPEI-CDs. The obtained hPEI-CDs were diluted to 10.0 mL solution with ultra-pure water and purified by the dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cutoff of 2 KDa for 24 h. Secondly, the EDTA-modified hPEI-CDs (denoted as hPEI-CD-EDTA) was obtained by introducing EDTAD into hPEI-CDs solution. The spontaneous crosslinking reaction between acid anhydride and amino group (‒NH2) from hPEI-CD surface benefits the yield of hPEI-CD-EDTA. In brief, 100 μL of hPEI-CDs and 10.0 mL of EDTAD (0.01 M) were mixed and stirred in carbonate buffer (0.1 M pH = 9.6) for 2 h, and then dialyzed for 48 h with a 1 KDa dialysis membrane to remove excess EDTA and carbonate. Finally, Eu3+ was linked onto the surface of hPEI-CD-EDTA via strong chelation affinity to produce hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanostructure. For preparing hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanostructure, 200 μL of 750 μM Eu3+ solution was mixed with 800 μL a sixth of hPEI-CD-EDTA solution under stirring. The product was collected after 2 h reaction and was dialyzed for 12 h with a 1 KDa dialysis membrane before use. All three solutions were stored at 4 °C for further use.

A stock solution of DPA (50 mM) was prepared and used to prepare standard solutions through a serial dilution. DPA detection was conducted with following protocol. Briefly, 50 μL solution with different DPA concentrations were added to 1.0 mL Tris-HCl buffer (pH = 9.0, 25 mM) diluted hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanoprobe solution (1/4 of original concentration), the final concentrations of DPA were 0, 1.0, 5.0, 10, 30, 50, 100, 300, 500, 800 and 1000 nM, respectively. After the mixed solution was sufficiently reacted for 1 min, the fluorescence spectra were collected by F-7000 at an excitation wavelength of 270 nm. Meanwhile, 400 nm long pass filter was used to eliminate the frequency-doubled interference. The following amino acids, organic acid and ions were used to evaluate the specificity of the hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanoprobe: Asp, Cys, Ala, Glu, BA, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cl−, and NO3 −. Accordingly, in order to further investigate the interference, 50 μL interferent solutions (2 μM) was added into 1.0 mL Tris-HCl buffer diluted hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanoprobe solution in the absence and presence of 100 nM DPA. After 1 min reaction, the fluorescence spectra of solutions were recorded with the same measurement settings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DPA sensing in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: The human serum sample obtained from healthy postulants. First, the sample was added equal volume acetonitrile to precipitate proteins. Then the sample was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min. The clear supernatant liquid was dried under vacuum drying and re-dispersed in ultrapure water to its original volume. Then, the re-dispersed human serum sample was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and diluted before analysis. Similarly, 50 μL serum solution with extra added DPA were introduced into to 1.0 mL Tris-HCl buffer (pH = 9.0, 25 mM) diluted hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanoprobe solution (1/4 of original concentration), the final concentrations of DPA were 0, 1.0, 5.0, 10, 30, 50, 100, 300, 500, 800 and 1000 nM, respectively. After the mixed solution was sufficiently reacted for 1 min, the fluorescence spectra were collected by F-7000 at an excitation wavelength of 270 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bacterial spore assay and screening of metabolic DPA-release
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, the noninfectious B. subtilis was used as B. anthracis simulacrum. According to the reported culture procedure [8], all sample tubes and related materials were firstly sterilized with alcohol under UV light. The subsequent culture experiments were performed in an operator under the protection of antigas mask, exposure suit and sterile disposable gloves. The sporulation of B. subtilis was conducted by an incubation of it for 3–4 day at 37 °C on an LB agar slant culture-medium (10.0 g/L tryptone, 3.0 g/L beef meal, 5.0 g/L NaCl). The B. subtilis spores were obtained by washing the culture 4–5 times with sterile ultrapure water after incubation of few days. The useless growth medium was removed through centrifugation. To produce DPA under the stimulation, a certain amount of bacterial samples was dispersed in 10 mM Ala solution. Then, the germination activation was conducted at 70 °C for 90 min. The resulted suspension was filtered through a 0.22 μM aqueous phase filter to remove bacteria or insoluble impurities before analysis. For screening of metabolic DPA release, 5.0 μL of the pretreated filtrate was added to the designed paper device. And the fluorescence image of metabolite accompanied paper device was recorded under UV light (254 nm) irradiation. All pictures in this work were obtained with a Camera+ app on an iPhone. The bacterial concentration was determined using a fast drying method. That is, a certain volume of pure bacterial sample (without medium) was dried in an oven at 90 °C until to constant weight, and weighed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanostructure
ce:section.ce:para: At the starting point, hPEI-CDs were firstly synthesized by a previously reported hydrothemal method [20,24]. As is known, there are a number of amino group (‒NH2) on the surface of hPEI-CDs. The hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ were prepared through a two-steps surface functionalization. Firstly, the EDTA-modified hPEI-CDs (denoted as hPEI-CD-EDTA) was obtained by introducing EDTAD into hPEI-CD solution. The spontaneous crosslinking reaction between acid anhydride and –NH2 yielded hPEI-CD-EDTA. Secondly, Eu3+ was linked onto the surface of hPEI-CD-EDTA via strong chelation to produce hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanostructure. According to the UV–vis absorption spectra, the hPEI-CDs displayed maximum fluorescence excitation and emission wavelengths at 350 and 440 nm [20,24], respectively (Fig. 2 A). Such fluorescence profiles are consistent with the previous work, implying the synthesis of hPEI-CDs. The TEM image showed that the hPEI-CDs possess spherical shape and uniform size dispersion with an average diameter of 2.3 ± 0.4 nm from 100 counts (Fig. 2B). According to the HRTEM image (Fig. S1), the lattice spacing is measured to be ~0.13 nm, which is consistent with the reported works [17,18]. It was seen that the hPEI-CDs after adding Eu3+ did not show a dramatic aggregation, revealing the great monodispersity (Fig. 2C). Meanwhile, the fluorescence emission spectra of hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ showed characteristic emission peaks centered at 580 nm, 594 nm, and 616 nm (Fig. S2), which belong to 5D0→7F0, 5D0→7F1, and 5D0→7F2 of Eu3+, respectively [2]. These result indicated the successful preparation of hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+.

To demonstrate the successful preparation of hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanostructure, other conventional characterizations, including Zeta potential, FT-IR, and time-resolved fluorescence spectra of hPEI-CDs at each stage, were performed. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the Zeta potential of hPEI-CDs changed from +10.5 mV to −8.2 mV and finally became +9.0 mV during the functionalization. This variation was due to the changed surface state of hPEI-CDs [25]. After the addition of EDTAD, numbers of positively charged amino groups (‒NH2) on the surface of hPEI-CDs changed to negatively charged carboxyl groups (‒COOH). However, with the addition of Eu3+, EDTA-Eu3+ chelation changed the surface charge from negative to positive again. In addition, such a surface variation was also proved by the FT-IR measurements. As shown in Fig. 3B, the N–H stretch band (~3400 cm−1) was observed in all stages, suggesting the preservation of hPEI-CDs during the functionalization [20]. After adding EDTAD, the new peaks centered at 1475 and 3520 cm−1 belong to –COO− and O–H stretch vibrations appeared, implying the generation of –COOH on the surface of hPEI-CDs [26]. With the addition of Eu3+, the –COO− stretch band shifted from 1475 to 1463 cm−1, suggesting the coordination of –COO− with Eu3+. Meanwhile, the characteristic peak of Eu‒O centered at 728 cm−1 arose, which further demonstrated the chelation between Eu3+ and surface EDTA [27,28].

According to the time-resolved fluorescence measurements (Fig. 3C), a first increase and subsequent decrease of hPEI-CD's lifetime was observed during the surface modification. The fluorescence lifetimes of hPEI-CDs were calculated through multiexponential decay fitting (Table S1). The lifetime of bare hPEI-CDs was 9.294 ns. However, it increased to 9.938 ns when the hPEI-CD surface was covered with newly generated –COOH. This slight increase might be assigned to the EDTA-induced surface passivation effect, which prevents the approaching of hPEI-CD surface from O2 or H2O molecules [23]. Interestingly, a distinct diminishment of lifetime to 7.816 ns was perceived upon the formation of hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanostructure. Since the surface –COOH is generated form chemical linking and is not involved in the emissive processes, we can rule out the Eu3+ chelation-mediated break-up of irradiative pathways of hPEI-CDs. In consideration of the characteristic fluorescence of Eu3+, a FRET mechanism between hPEI-CDs and Eu3+ was suspected [20]. Such a hypothesis was firstly verified by the partial overlap between excitation spectrum of Eu3+ and the fluorescence emission spectrum of hPEI-CDs (Fig. S3A). In addition, the addition of Eu3+ led to a decreased blue emission (440 nm) and amplified red emission (616 nm) in comparison to the sole hPEI-CDs-EDTA, sole Eu3+ or EDTA-Eu3+solution (Fig. S3B), meaning the occurrence of FRET [29]. Moreover, Eu3+ was linked to the hPEI-CDs surface through chelation, making the distance between them far less than 10 nm. The close distance also facilitates the energy transfer between them. Therefore, we can conclude that the Eu3+-induced lifetime decrease of hPEI-CDs was due to the occurrence of FRET.

To further prove the successful construction of hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanostructure, whole XPS spectrum of the final product was performed. As shown in Fig. 3D, four binding energy peaks centered at 298.5, 414.5, 545.0, and 1151.0 were observed. These peaks belong to C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and Eu 3d, respectively. The Eu 3d XPS spectrum (Fig. S4A) displayed two peaks at 1136 eV and 1164 eV, assigning to Eu 3d5/2 and Eu 3d3/2, respectively [7]. Additionally, the C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S4B) could be divided into three peaks (284.7 eV, 286.1 eV and 288.1 eV), meaning that CC/C–C, C–O/C–N and CO bonds are present in hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanostructure [19]. Moreover, the N 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S4C) clearly exhibited two peaks around 399.5 and 401.2 eV, implying the existence of C–N bond and C–NH bond. And the proportion of C–NH bonds was dramatically higher than that of C–N bond, probably because of the amine-rich structure of hPEI molecule. The oxygen content in hPEI-CDs was calculated to be 22.7%, while it became 28.4% in hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ (Fig. S4D). The increased oxygen content was due to the existence of surface EDTA that has abundant oxygen element. This also demonstrated the successful modification of EDTA onto the hPEI-CD surface. Furthermore, the absence of peak at 1150.0 eV in the XPS spectrum of hPEI-CDs also demonstrated the successful preparation of hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanostructure via surface modification strategy. Taken all data together, the hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanostructure with dual-emission character was successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of ratiometric DPA sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: It is reported that EDTA can greatly promote the intrinsic emission of Eu3+ by effectively preventing the approaching of –OH or H2O [2,23]. Such design is usually applied to improve the detection sensitivity of Eu3+-based sensing probes. In this work, we attempted to construct a ratiometric nanprobe using the dual-emissive hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanostructure. Taking the specific and strong chelation affinity between Eu3+ and DPA into consideration, we expect that the hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanostructure can act as nanoprobe for detecting DPA with high sensitivity.

The as-prepared hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanoprobe are inherently water-soluble because of the abundant –NH2 and –COOH groups on the surface. Since the Eu3+/DPA chelation plays an important role on the generation of red-emission, parameters (e.g., surface Eu3+ content, solution pH, and reaction time, etc.) that affect the chelation may have effects on the DPA detection sensitivity. In this work, a systematic study was conducted to optimize those experimental conditions by monitoring the fluorescence intensity ratio at 616 nm. It is expected that the concentration of Eu3+ is significant for the sensitivity of DPA detection, because extra Eu3+ that integrates with –NH2 only produces weakly fluorescent complex after chelating with DPA. In addition, excess Eu3+ can lead to the aggregation of hPEI-CDs. In this case, a low sensitivity toward DPA detection would be obtained. Here, we investigated the effect of Eu3+ concentration on the DPA sensing. The fluorescence spectra of hPEI-CD-EDTA were recorded upon adding a series of concentrations of Eu3+ (final concentration: 19, 38, 75, 113, 150, 225, 300, 375, 750 and 1500 μM) in the presence of 500 μM DPA (final concentration). As illustrated in Fig. S5A and 5B, the red-emission increased from 19 to 150 μM. This indicated that Eu3+ in this concentration range was completely “captured” by surface EDTA, and the fluorescence intensity linearly increased with the increased concentrations of Eu3+. Notice that non-linearly increased fluorescence was observed when the concentration of Eu3+ was up to 225 μM, indicating the presence of superfluous Eu3+, which only chelates with –NH2 and/or DPA. Considering the fixed chelation ratio between EDTA and Eu3+, the concentration of Eu3+ could be used for determine EDTA ligand density on the surface of hPEI-CDs. The total EDTA on CDs (200 μL stock solution) is 150 nmol. The mass concentration of hPEI-CDs solution was measured to be 0.336 mg/mL. Therefore, the EDTA density on the surface of CD was calculated to be ~3 per CD.

The fluorescence, however, rapidly decreased when the concentration of Eu3+ was higher than 225 μM, and plateaued over 750 μM. According to Fig. S5B, the addition of excess Eu3+ could result in dramatic fluorescence quenching of hPEI-CDs. Also, the solution seemed a little cloudy upon adding high concentrations of Eu3+. These results suggested that the decreased fluorescence at high concentration of Eu3+ was mainly due to the aggregation of hPEI-CDs. Thus, we chose the concentration of Eu3+ at 150 μM for the construction of dual-emissive hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanoprobe.

In addition, the reaction-time is also an important factor towards the chelation. To understand the reaction rate of the proposed nanoprobe, the time-dependent fluorescence at 616 nm of hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ toward 2.0 μM DPA was monitored. As shown in Fig. S6, the fluorescence increased quickly once DPA was added. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity reached a flatform after 30 s reaction, indicating the ultra-rapid response. Such a short reaction time may promise the possibility of rapid DPA detection in complicated samples. To ensure the complete reaction, all fluorescence spectra were recorded after 1 min reaction.

As is known, the chelation affinity is usually related to the solution pH. In order to realize the rapid and sensitive DPA detection, the effect of the pH on the proposed nanoprobe was studied. We recorded the fluorescence intensity ratio of 616 nm and 440 nm (I616/I440) as the signal reporter. Such values of hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanoprobe solution were monitored under the pH range from 2.0 to 11.0 with the addition of 1.0 μM DPA. As illustrated in Fig. S7, the optimal pH was determined to be 9.0. However, when pH was greater than 9.0, a quick decrease in the fluorescence intensity was observed, which might be attributed to the hydrolysis of Eu3+ and the formation of Eu(OH)3. Accordingly, all subsequent sensing experiments were conducted in pH 9.0 and 25 mM HCl-Tris buffer.

After adding DPA to the hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ solution, the red emission was noticeably increased while the blue emission was nearly unchanged. The bright fluorescence and dual-emission characters made the hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanostructure as a promising nanoprobe for ratiometric DPA sensing. For the sensitivity study, the fluorescence emission spectra of hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanoprobe solution were recorded after adding DPA with different concentrations. It can be seen that in Fig. 4 A, three characteristic emission peaks centered at 580, 594, and 616 nm gradually increased as increasing DPA concentrations from 0 to 1000 nM. Despite of the increment of all three emission peaks, the emission at 616 nm showed the highest fluorescence. It means higher sensitivity would be realized if this signal is used as the reporter. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm from hPEI-CDs remained, indicating that this blue-emission can act as ideal internal reference. Therefore, the fluorescence ratio of I616/I440 was utilized to indicate DPA concentration. The I616/I440 exhibited a good linear relationship (R 2 = 0.998) versus the DPA concentration ranging from 1 to 100 nM (Fig. 4A inset), and could be easily described by an equation: I616/I440 = 0.0735 + K[Q], where K is the fluorescence enhancement constant, and [Q] is the concentration of DPA. K was calculated to be 2.0 × 106 M−1 by linear regression of the plots. The LOD was calculated to be 190 pM (S/N = 3). The possible reasons for favorable sensitivity might be attributed to the dual-emission character and EDTA-enhanced Eu3+ fluorescence upon chelating with DPA. To understand the importance of dual-emission character on DPA sensing, we performed the DPA detection with EDTA-Eu3+ probe. As shown in Fig. S8, the LOD toward DPA was calculated to be 21 nM (R2 = 0.987) in EDTA-Eu3+ system. In addition, the fluorescence intensity at same DPA using hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ was much higher than that of EDTA-Eu3+. The strong fluorescence and high sensitivity proved the importance of dual-emission character of hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanoprobe. In order to demonstrate the stability of proposed nanoprobe, the reproducibility studies of the hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ solution were investigated. Twenty repeated measurements of fluorescence response-induced by 1 μM DPA were utilized to verify the stability of the proposed method. As illustrated in Fig. S9, the low relative standard deviation (2.43%) of I616/I440 ratio demonstrated that the designed hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ FRET nanoprobe was highly reproducible.

Since Bacillus anthracis is very dangerous for living organisms, we cann't perform real infectious sample for the test. In order to demonstrate the practical application of DPA sensing in biological samples, we conducted the DPA detection using the proposed nanoprobes in human serum samples via strand addition method. In order to avoid the interference of other substances, the pretreatment of human serum was conducted to remove biomacromolecules, i.e., proteins. As shown in Fig. S10, DPA sensing was realized in human serum sample with high sensitivity (LOD = 1 nM). The high sensitivity suggests that the proposed nanoprobes are capable of DPA sensing in complicated biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ponit-of-care DPA detection with paper device
ce:section.ce:para: In comparison with spectroscopic detection strategy, visual sensing of biological DPA is more significant to health diagnosis. In recent years, visual point-of-care testing techniques have been undergoing rapid development because of their advantages of low cost, practicability, and miniaturization [36,37]. As one of the mostly common substrates, paper-based point-of-care testing device attracts growing attention and has been applied to rapid and visual analysis of chemical and/or biological targets [38]. For the development of an effective point-of-care DPA testing paper device, the production of hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanoprobe-loaded paper with low autofluorescence and high nanoprobe dispersity is essential. Since the ordinary testing papers have non-negligible autofluorescence and large micropore, PVDF film was used as the substrate. As referred in previous work, PVDF film shows ignorable autofluorescence and small micropores inside its highly stable and compact structure [39]. The hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanoprobe was dispersed onto the surface of PVDF film using ink-jet printing technique to eliminate the nonhomogeneity [40].

The mechanism of visual DPA detection with naked-eye was illustrated in Fig. 5 A. To verify the visual DPA detection with the prepared paper device, the fluorescence images of device were obtained upon adding various concentrations of DPA solution. For the test, 5.0 μL DPA solutions (from 0.5 to 500 μM) were added onto the PVDF film for 1 min reaction. As expected, with the increasing concentrations of DPA, the fluorescence color of paper device under 254 nm UV light irradiation gradually changed from blue to purple and red (Fig. 5B), suggesting the successful visual DPA detection using the designed paper device. The LOD was confirmed as 500 nM. The rapid response and sensitive DPA visualization made the hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanoprobe-loaded PVDF paper device adaptable for practical point-of-care DPA sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualization of DPA release from B. subtilis spore germination
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the practical application of the designed paper device, we tried to detect metabolic DPA from bacterial spores. In this work, B. subtilis was used as the noninfectious B. anthracis simulacrum. The production of metabolic DPA was performed through a standard incubation and stimulation procedure as described in Fig. 6 A. The production of DPA in bacterial metabolites was firstly tested using the hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ nanoprobe solution. As shown in Fig. 6B, neither spores nor Ala could cause distinct fluorescence change. However, thermal stimulation of spores in the presence of Ala resulted in dramatic fluorescence increment, indicating the release of DPA. To further evaluate the visual DPA detection ability of the designed paper device, bacterial metabolites with different bacterial spores were tested. According to Fig. 6C, the blue-emissive paper device under UV light irradiation gradually turned to purple with the increasing concentrations of bacterial spores, suggesting the capable metabolic DPA sensing using the designed paper device. By integrating with heat-germination approach, a 1.866 μg/mL of bacterial spore was able to be identified with naked eyes. The successful bacterial metabolic DPA detection implies that the proposed paper device is capable of practical bacteria detection in complicated biological systems.

In summary, hPEI-CD-EDTA-Eu3+ FRET nanoprobe for ratiometric DPA detection has been developed by employing hPEI-CD as the internal reference and EDTA-Eu3+ chelating complex on the surface as the sensing element. The strong chelation affinity between Eu3+ and DPA endows the completion of reaction within 30 s, which greatly shortens the parsing time. Meanwhile, the self-calibration character also makes the nanoprobe sensitive and accurate. The proposed nanoprobe shows excellent selectivity toward DPA over other amino acids, anions or metal ions. Furthermore, the point-of-care DPA visualization is achieved using nanoprobe-loaded PVDF paper device. More importantly, screening of DPA release during the germination of B. subtilis spores stimulated by Ala is successfully realized. This work also demonstrates an interesting combination of ratiometric sensing and specific chelation for sensitive biomarker analysis. Thus, new avenues for the design of versatile methods for the rapid visualization of harmful/pathogenic bacterial spores based on a similar tactic are now possible in food or life-science related fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21605003, 21521005 and 21575010), the National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program, No. 2014CB932103), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (buctrc201619), and the Open Research Fund Program of Beijing Key Lab of Plant Resource Research and Development, Beijing Technology and Business University (PRRD-2018-YB3).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2019.120368.
